paragraphs.body: Possessing the "Threads" logo for over a decade, Threads Software Limited, a small company in the UK, has warned Meta of future lawsuits about using the name. Proving an "official press release", the company's managing director, John Yardley, mentioned, "Taking on a U.S. $150 billion company is not an easy decision for us to make. Our business now faces a serious threat from one of the largest technology companies in the world." By the time Meta's Threads was launched in July 2023, Threads Software's Facebook account was found to be closed. [1]
The unfair situation of keeping danger out of their "trademark" between small and large companies was shown in the dispute.[2]

Threads Software Ltd.'s Point of View[edit] The company claimed that the "threads.app" domain was not for sale and refused Meta's bid. Threads Software Ltd. informed Meta of requesting "an injunction in the UK High Court" except if Meta stopped using the name "Threads". John Yardley, the company's managing director, said that Meta planned to "incur legal costs" just to keep the rights of Threads Software Ltd.'s existence. Moreover, the actions and the bizarre categories in the law of trademark protection could give Meta the chance to postpone the litigation.[2]

Threads Software Ltd.[edit] Located in Surbiton, England, the software company aims to support business communication with an organized dashboard. Threads provide help "From intelligent search functions to phone call transcription and HubSpot integration."[3] Its software was licensed around the world and used by approximately 1000 organizations.

Threads (Message hub)[edit] Provided by Threads Software Ltd., Threads is an "intelligent message hub" and was "convinced and trademarked in 2012 by JPY Ltd." There is a chance of leading toward confusion if Meta takes the name "Threads".[2]

Meta's Point of View[edit] Meta stated that "Threads Software does not appear to be software for the extraction of business information and knowledge." In addition, "the trademark has been registered for more than five years it can be removed from the register on the grounds of non-use" was also mentioned.[2]

annotations.text: "Threads Software does not appear to be software for the extraction of business information and knowledge." In addition, "the trademark has been registered for more than five years it can be removed from the register on the grounds of non-use"
annotations.comment: Please reduce the use of long direct quotes and paraphrase the information in your own words.

paragraphs.body: The reality of the company (Threads Software Limited) under Zuckerberg's perspective[edit] Zuckerberg mentioned that Meta owned much more money and lawyers, making it harder for the small company to win the battle in the U.S. than in the British court. He also said that "part of that is because the U.S. has more rules surrounding how you make a determination of an infringement. It’s less likely that in the United States that anyone has heard of this small British company." Zuckerberg made the point that it has a low chance of hurting Meta. He believes that the users of Meta's Threads will continue to use the app with the updating of more and more functions and features.[1]

annotations.comment: I would suggest using a neutral tone throughout the paper without sharing personal opinions.

paragraphs.body: The growth of Meta's Threads and the problem it faced[edit] On July 5, 2023, Meta's Threads was officially opened to the public. The new app was "launched as a competitor to X, formerly known as Twitter." [1] Instagram users can access the new app by logging in to their accounts. Over 100 million people started to use the app within five days.[4] Meanwhile, the app is still inaccessible in Europe, waiting for the European Commission to go through "data policies" and give out the "regulatory clearance".[1]

The declining amount of users[edit] The "fastest-growing platform in the history" faced a rapid decrease in the number of users. Meta's Threads faced the challenge of losing 80% of its users and was criticized by a British content writer for social media agency, Joseph Rudd, for the lack of creators, influencers, and creator analytics.[1]

Wrath of other competitors[edit] As the owner of X, Elon Musk challenged Meta by stating that there is an issue in sneaking "trade secret." He claimed that Zuckerberg persuaded former workers from X to assist in constructing a "Twitter clone."[1]

American Threads, a women's fashion brand, forced Meta to change the name to "@threadsapp" in their Instagram account when Meta's Threads first launched. The dissension faded after the retailer renewed its account username.[1]^

2. a b c d Peet, Ellen (2024-02-29). "Threads Software v. Meta: trademark dispute sends small business into a spin". Trademark Lawyer Magazine. Retrieved 2025-02-26. 3. ^ "Shared Inbox Software For Teams - Threads Intelligent Message Hub". Threads. Retrieved 2025-02-26. 4. ^ "Threads: The inside story of Meta's newest social app". Engineering at Meta. 2023-09-07. Retrieved 2025-02-26.
